content_preview.title: Soil Fungi Borrowed a Bacterial Gene Millions of Years Ago to Freeze Water, Opening a Path to Safer Cloud Seeding
content_preview.summary: A Science Advances study identifies cell-free ice-nucleating proteins in Mortierellaceae soil fungi, acquired from bacteria via horizontal gene transfer, offering a non-toxic alternative to silver iodide for cloud seeding.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## A Hidden Talent in Common Soil Fungi

Scientists have known for decades that certain soil-dwelling fungi can trigger ice formation at temperatures well above the point at which pure water freezes on its own. What they did not know was how the fungi pulled it off. A [study published in Science Advances on March 11, 2026](https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/sciadv.aed9652), led by Rosemary J. Eufemio and an international team spanning Virginia Tech, Boise State University, the University of Uta...

editor_notes:
Excellent science journalism covering a genuine research breakthrough. The article clearly explains complex biology (horizontal gene transfer, ice nucleation proteins) for a general audience while maintaining scientific accuracy. Summary accurately reflects content. Word count (941) appropriate for News category. The automated review successfully fetched phys.org and PNAS via Archive.org fallback, confirming these sources exist and are accessible.

source_verification:
- science.org (Science Advances): HTTP 403. Primary peer-reviewed paper. DOI pattern (10.1126/sciadv.aed9652) is consistent with legitimate Science Advances publication. Cannot independently verify content directly but claims are corroborated by secondary source.
- phys.org: HTTP 403 on direct fetch but successfully retrieved via Archive.org during automated review. Source snapshot captured. Article covers the same research and is the source for Vinatzer quotes about cloud seeding and gene transfer rarity.
- pnas.org (PNAS 2023): HTTP 403 on direct fetch but successfully retrieved via Archive.org during automated review. DOI pattern (10.1073/pnas.2303243120) is legitimate. Referenced as prior work establishing molecular basis of fungal ice nucleation.

All three sources are from reputable scientific publishers (AAAS, Phys.org, National Academy of Sciences). Academic publisher bot-blocking is standard practice and does not indicate source invalidity.
